blocks.content: He picked the camera back up.

She watched him do it — the reflexive reach, the phone raised, the eye that never stopped seeing things worth keeping — and she didn't ask him to put it down. This was who he was. This was the language he loved in. She had known that since she was eight years old.
He filmed the field. The fireflies. The dark edge of the tree line.
Then he turned the camera toward her.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You look — I want to remember how you look right now.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I've been crying for four days.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I know. That's part of it.

She looked at the camera the way she'd learned to — not performing, not posing, just being looked at. He'd told her once that there was a difference between someone who knew they were being filmed and someone who simply let themselves be seen. She'd learned to be the second one.
She looked at the lens. Behind it, his face. Behind his face, everything.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: What are you going to do with all this footage.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Give it to our kid. One day.

He lowered the phone. They stood together in the field until the cold came in off the trees and they couldn't ignore it anymore. Then they walked home — back through the dark streets, back to the house with the lights left on, back to the life that was waiting for them to carry it forward.
Behind them, the field went dark.
